title: Returning to the Apocalyptic Wasteland of Fallen Land
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OHt_00p2dmY

Four years on, the first video of 2023 finds Daniel (@dungeondive) breaking a long Fallen Land hiatus. He'd been deferring the game while waiting for a second edition; he gave up waiting, got it back to the table, and remembered why he loves it. He also confesses it should have been on the [Top 50](https://dungeondive.quest/t/1586) and got forgotten — an oversight already flagged in the postmortem.

Most of the video is a structured *five things I love* tour, played around a real game in progress. (1) The rulebook — "a perfect example of how to do a rulebook," indexed, exemplified, with a six-page detailed turn analysis at the back. (2) The art — amateurish, charming, the doodled-on-the-back-of-a-school-notebook aesthetic that ties straight back to the Arthur's Toys story from the 2019 video. (3) The encounter decks — four area decks plus a missions deck, with writing strong enough to carry the analytical/narrative load. (4) The game is *easy and fun* — a soft point of disagreement with viewers who want it harder, and Daniel's preferred fix (occasional self-inflicted red-dot draws) is offered as a compromise. (5) The character/loot interactions — link bonuses for couples and matched gear that reward the bookkeeping.

His one apprehension about the looming second edition: that it'll outgrow the small box. Everything currently fits — barely — and the epic-in-a-shoebox quality is half the charm.

----

Daniel reframes Fallen Land's solo difficulty as a feature: it's a game *about adventure*, not a game about being beaten up by adventure. Where do you sit on the threat-management vs. emergent-narrative axis when you're playing solo?
